**Part I: The Feeling**

# Two Cities

---

Something mattered enough that you drove across town. You found parking. You sat in an uncomfortable chair for two hours, through forty-five minutes of business that wasn't yours. Your item came up. You had three minutes at the mic.

You spoke. Maybe your voice shook a little — it takes something to stand up in a room like that. You said something true. You used your three minutes well.

Then you went home.

The decision went ahead, as it had already been planned.

Your effort was real — so what happened to it? Maybe your good-faith participation changed the outcome. Maybe it didn't. But the one thing it reliably produced was legitimacy.

---

Legitimacy is the one thing institutions can't manufacture for themselves. A decision made in public, with community input, is harder to challenge. It has armor. The armor is made from your participation.

You produced the armor. You handed it to them. You risked a parking ticket.

The system didn't deceive you. It ran the process correctly and gave you your three minutes. But amongst all the debate and procedure, the function of the system is not to arrive at the correct result. It is to bestow legitimacy on whatever the result is.

The word for this is capture. Not corruption — capture. The city didn't steal your participation. It absorbed it — the way healthy soil does with fallen leaves: gathering the energy, converting it into the institution's armor.

---

Atlantic City was capture at full force; most days, it looks like the meeting you just attended.

A neighborhood board is a mechanism for converting community energy into institutional legitimacy. When you show up and speak, you generate engagement, knowledge, consent — the raw material for the appearance of democratic participation. The board is the interface. Your input enters and is processed into a form the institution can use.

What comes back out is usually much less than what went in.

This is not unique to Norfolk or to neighborhood governance. It is the operating logic of any process designed to absorb your participation rather than transmit it into power. The petition that gets added to your email list. The public comment that goes into the record and changes nothing. The community meeting that produces a report that produces another community meeting. The form repeats until it conditions reality, never producing change.

It's not that the appearance of democratic participation has replaced the thing. It's that we can no longer tell them apart. Real wealth, or debt? Real connection, or self-promotion? Real productivity, or its performance? The signs of the thing are the signs of the performance.

If you think all you have is your voice, what you're missing is called leverage. A tenant association can call a rent strike — that's leverage. A community land trust uses ownership against the speculative market — that's leverage. An organized neighborhood has the stamina to occupy the city's time and resources, directing them at the problems it needs solved — that's leverage becoming capacity.

You signed the petition because you cared. You showed up at the hearing because you were committed. The structure you were engaging with was designed to absorb that energy, not multiply it into change.

Ask this of everything: Does this build relationships, capacity, knowledge that persist? Does it leave something the next person can inherit? Do we walk away from this action with something in common that we can build on?

---

The Atlantic City neighborhood was demolished in 1957. The campus that replaced it is still there. In 2021, in the midst of a federal Fair Housing lawsuit, the city was simultaneously demolishing Tidewater Gardens — different community, same machine — and promising that this time, things would be different.

"When I hear, 'This time things will be different,' I think it's malarkey," said Zenobia Wilson, displaced from Tidewater Gardens in 2022. Her grandmother had been displaced from Huntersville decades earlier. "They're saying it's for us, but really they're just sprinkling us in there to make us feel welcome."

She knows the structural relationship. She has learned it through her grandmother's experience and through her own. She cannot stop the demolition. But she can refuse to provide the legitimacy it needs by pretending the promises are credible.

That refusal — the naming, the seeing clearly, the refusal to call the loss progress — is not nothing.

It is where the work begins.

---

Three years later, in a different city. A fish market had just closed. The vendors were still cleaning up — hosing down the stalls, boxing what was left, the smell of the sea fading slowly from the pavement. It was early evening in the Gothic quarter.

A man was already there, setting up. Not a cargo bike, not a van — a custom rig, everything self-contained: the speakers mounted low, the battery tucked in, the mixer fixed to the handlebars. No truck. No generator. No permit required, probably. He set up in the street outside the market in the time it takes to unpack a bag.

Then — salsa.

Not a performance — a class, or something between a class and a party, the boundary dissolving quickly. People of all ages. Couples, groups of friends, laughter, a few people alone at the edges bobbing along, already getting drawn in. The permanent covered stalls along the street sold something cold and sweet. The street, which an hour earlier had been about fish, was now about dancing, and nobody had organized this except in the loosest possible sense — someone had brought a bike and a sound system and an idea, and the city had made the rest possible. Music made the street a commons — evoked it, not organized it: the oldest magic, felt before it is understood.

Barcelona has been transforming its intersections for years. Taking back space that was given to cars in the 1960s and 1970s, returning it to the people who live on the streets that those cars cut through. The city calls them *superilles* — clusters of streets deprioritized for through traffic, freed up for walking, cycling, planting, sitting, dancing. Rupture, at municipal scale — the car's code broken on the street, the street turned toward the commons. The policy was controversial when it started.

The infrastructure had been changed, the space had been returned, and what people did with it was exactly what you would expect people to do when given a street and an evening: they made something together that none of them could have made alone. Tuesday-night practice: the new world danced as if it had always been here.

On a Tuesday. In January.

I watched from the edge. I'm a DJ. I noticed the rig. The music had started before I got there — it wasn't waiting for me, or for anyone. There was ease in that. The ease of knowing that life goes on even if yours doesn't.

Barcelona has also been running out of water. The reservoirs that supply the city have been at critical levels, some years below twenty percent of capacity. The drought is structural — the Mediterranean is warming, the precipitation patterns are shifting, the snowpack that feeds the rivers is thinner each decade. The city that has figured out how to return streets to people is also a city learning, under pressure, that it belongs to a commons larger than any municipal government can govern: the planet's hydrological cycle, the atmosphere, the climate that no one owns and everyone depends on.

The party in the street and the water crisis are the same story. The question of who governs shared resources — who decides what the street is for, who decides how the water is used, who bears the costs of the decisions and who captures the benefits — is one question, asked at different scales. Barcelona is getting better at asking it. Most cities aren't asking it at all.

We spent three days there. We didn't take the metro once. The city gave itself to walking.

---

Why Barcelona? There are reasons.

The deep memory has a name: the CNT. The Confederación Nacional del Trabajo — anarcho-syndicalist, federated, assembly-based; at its 1930s peak, over a million members in Catalonia. The CNT-FAI ran Barcelona in 1936. Taking it down took fascists and Communists, both — Franco's army from outside the Republic, and inside the Republic the Stalinist-aligned forces attacking the anarchist and POUM positions in Barcelona's May Days of 1937. Different uniforms, same intention. By the time the war ended, what was left of the CNT was driven underground, killed off, scattered into exile.

The form survived. Murray Bookchin took his lessons from it and called the practice *libertarian municipalism*. The Barcelona coalitions of the twenty-first century take theirs from the same place.

The CNT was Spain's part of a wider European left of the 1920s and 30s — Gramsci writing in a Mussolini prison, Reich in Berlin naming the somatic-political fascism of the era. The CNT was the part of that generation that built the most actual power. They lost by being defeated, not by being wrong.

Catalonia has outlasted most of the empires it has been part of, by not being one itself.

The Crown of Aragon did project Mediterranean power in the late medieval period — briefly controlling Sicily, Sardinia, even Naples for a few decades under Alfonso V. But the political imagination that survived into modernity, especially after the Bourbon erasure of Catalan autonomy in 1714, organized around something else: a region, a language, a working-class urban culture, a confederal tradition. Never an imperial actor. A constituent of empires, never their center.

The empires that contained it have fallen. The Holy Roman Empire, gone. The Habsburg-Spanish empire, gone. The Bourbon centralization undone by 1978. What persists is the city, the language, the practices, the relationship of people to land and to each other.

Barcelona's coalitions of the twenty-first century stand on this. They have a city, not a metropole; a region, not an empire. That has been enough.

Franco died in 1975. What followed was not revolution but constitutional design.

The 1978 Spanish Constitution, ratified in referendum, established a parliamentary monarchy and a system of *autonomous communities*: power devolved to seventeen regional governments, each with its own statute, parliament, and competencies. Catalonia got its 1979 Statute of Autonomy — education, language policy, health, urban planning, much of policing and cultural life. Spain became a quasi-federal state by another name.

This was the institutional opening. A city suppressed for forty years now had a regional government that could be its own argument with Madrid. The Catalan language returned to schools, signs, and television. The Generalitat — the historic Catalan government, dissolved by Franco in 1939 — was restored.

The transition wasn't perfect. It preserved much of the Franco-era state apparatus, kept the king the regime had positioned, gave amnesty to officials who had committed atrocities. Spanish democracy carries the compromise. But the autonomous community structure is real, and Barcelona's twenty-first-century coalitions could only happen in the space it opened.

This was also the moment of Negri and Deleuze-Guattari — the post-1968 left thinking past party politics toward distributed power, multitude, the political subject as a composition of singularities. The death of Franco landed in that moment. Spain wrote its new constitution into a theoretical environment already imagining new forms of political subjecthood.

Participatory budgeting deserves its own naming. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre invented the form in 1989: residents of a neighborhood decide directly what portion of the municipal budget gets spent on what. Money allocated by the people who will live with the consequences. Not a town hall meeting where people speak and then officials decide — a real allocation, binding on the city.

The form spread. Porto Alegre's experiment was studied, copied, adapted across dozens of cities globally. By the early 2000s it was a known practice with measurable results: reduced corruption, more equitable infrastructure spending, higher civic participation. The tool existed. It was waiting.

When Barcelona's coalition came to power, it had a tool kit. In 2017, the city built the Decidim platform and released it as open source — federated, modular, available to any city that wanted to use it. The tool turned: built to be given away. Decidim gave participatory budgeting digital scale: every neighborhood could deliberate, propose, vote. Even small allocations changed something. Practice the thing and you become someone for whom the thing is normal.

By 2014, Barcelona had what the rest of Spain did not yet have: a coalition ready to govern.

The 15M movement — the squares movement of 2011, when Spaniards by the millions occupied plazas to refuse the post-2008 austerity consensus — had not produced a party. It produced something stranger: a culture of assembly. Culture in the fermenter’s sense — a living thing, particular to its vessel, useless without a substrate to work on.

Occupy Wall Street erupted in the same year on the same impulse, and produced the same thing. In the United States, the culture of assembly dissolved without leaving an institutional shadow — Zuccotti Park cleared by police. In Spain, most of the assemblies also dissolved. But in a few places they crystallized into something durable. The difference was not the will of the people in the parks. It was the ground the parks sat on. The same starter, poured onto different ground. You can smell the difference in soil before you can say it — freshness or rot, ripeness or desiccation. One ground had been fed for decades: turned, inoculated by every generation of practice that survived in it. The other had been paved.

Barcelona en Comú — Barcelona in Common — was born from that crystallization. It was not a party. It was a rhizome — a citizen platform built bottom-up from neighborhood assemblies, sending shoots up wherever the soil was good: PAH activists, Podemos cadres, ecologist groups, neighborhood associations, the green-left, independents who had never run for anything. The platform asked its assemblies what the program should be. The assemblies decided.

This is what years of organizing infrastructure make possible: when the political opening came, the coalition was already built. The CNT memory, the autonomous community framework, the participatory practice — these had not been waiting. They had been preparing. The lineage acting in the present, summoned by the opening.

Barcelona has a peculiar financial position for a major European city: there is no stock exchange of its own to speak of. The historical Bolsa de Barcelona was folded into the integrated Spanish exchanges; the real capital markets sit in Madrid. Barcelona's economic substrate is equity and business — ownership of small and medium enterprises, the deep network of family firms, the port, manufacturing, the design industries, the cooperative sector, the tourist economy. Not securities. Not the financial extraction that produced Zurich or Frankfurt or captured Geneva.

This is a different shape of wealth. The capital is in the workshops and on the streets, not in trading floors.

What Barcelona has instead, and has built deliberately since the 1992 Olympics, is biopolitical capital. Quality of life. Climate. The walkable city. The plazas built around old trees. The plane trees over the boulevards. The food. The reputation. People — and increasingly the global pool of mobile knowledge workers, designers, scientists — want to be in Barcelona. The city commands mind share. The mind share converts into capital without going through a stock exchange.

This is its own kind of vulnerability — gentrification, tourism strangulation, the housing market pressed by short-term rentals — but it is not the vulnerability of a financialized city. A different power, attached to a different vulnerability, with different room to act.

Ada Colau was a housing activist before she was a mayor.

She helped found the PAH — the Plataforma de Afectados por la Hipoteca — in 2009, in the wreckage of the 2008 crisis, when Spanish banks were evicting families they had just sold variable-rate loans to. For five years she organized eviction blockades: stood in doorways, brought cameras, refused to let people be put out. The work made her a public face for an entire sector of dispossessed Spain.

In 2014, when the coalition formed around her, she ran for mayor. She had never held office. She was just past forty. She had been arrested at protests and bailed out by other activists. She had no political career to protect.

In 2015, she won.

What she brought to office was not the political class's view of itself. It was the view from the doorways. The eight years of her mayoralty — re-elected 2019, narrowly lost 2023 — were governed in the cadence of a person who had done direct work. The participatory budgets, the assembly networks, the housing reforms — these were not technocratic features. They were the practice of someone who had spent years refusing to make peace with the city's structural cruelty, now with the keys to the building.

This kind of coalition is not generated from nothing. Barcelona's working class has been organizing itself for a long time, and the city remembers.

In 1936, when Franco's forces moved against the elected government, Barcelona was the place where the people most decisively did not let them in. Anarchist unions and antifascist militias ran the city. Factories were collectivized. The trams kept running, the trains kept running, the bread kept getting baked — under workers' councils, not bosses. It was a real social revolution, and Franco needed three years and the collapse of international solidarity to defeat it. The city fell in January 1939.

What followed was forty years of dictatorship: executions, exile, the Catalan language banned in schools and the press, neighborhood organizations driven underground. But the memory did not die. It survived in kitchen tables, in songs, in the persistence of the language under suppression, in the slow rebuilding of cultural infrastructure after Franco's death in 1975.

When the assemblies of Barcelona en Comú began meeting in 2014, they were not inventing a new form. They were activating an old one. The neighborhood assemblies of the twenty-first century rhyme with the workers' assemblies of 1936 because they come out of the same city, the same memory, the same refusal.

Paris has been doing this too. Anne Hidalgo has been mayor since 2014 — almost exactly Colau's tenure. She has closed the Seine quays to cars. She has pushed the fifteen-minute city, removed parking spaces by the thousand, and built bicycle infrastructure where before there was only traffic. Paris on a Sunday in central arrondissements is recognizably different from Paris a decade ago — pedestrians on the Seine quays where the cars used to be.

Hidalgo was born in Spain. Her family came to France from Franco's Spain when she was a child. She knows the lineage. But her position is not Colau's position, and the contrast is the lesson.

Hidalgo governs inside the French centralized state. The mayor of Paris is constrained by national authority in ways the mayor of Barcelona — operating inside the Catalan autonomous community — is not. Her political base is the French Socialist Party, not a citizen platform built from assemblies. She is a career politician advancing a progressive program from inside an existing party, against opposition from drivers and conservatives and the right press, and the program has produced real change. But the next mayor can put the parking spaces back. The structure that produced Hidalgo can also produce her opposite.

What Colau had — what Hidalgo does not — is a coalition rooted in the neighborhoods that elected her, with cultural infrastructure older than her mayoralty and longer-lived than it. The Barcelona that Colau changed has an immune system. What Hidalgo is building in Paris is a different kind of power — vulnerable in a different way.

The clearest contrast is Naples.

Napoli is the same kind of place as Barcelona in the ways that matter: a Mediterranean port city, working-class spine, anarchist and socialist traditions, a deep food culture, a population that has lived under and survived various empires. By the metrics that should produce a Barcelona, Naples should be one too.

It is not. Naples is the place people leave. The population of the metropolitan area has been declining for decades; young Italians from the South flee north — to Milan, Turin, Rome — for work that does not exist at home. Barcelona is the inverse: the place people come to. Returning Catalans, Spaniards from inland regions where opportunity has collapsed, mobile workers from across Europe.

The contrast cuts deeper. Naples hosts the United States Sixth Fleet — Naval Support Activity Naples, operational headquarters for American military presence across Europe and Africa, in place since the 1950s. Imagine the United States Navy basing the Sixth Fleet in Barcelona. You cannot. The two cities are positioned in the global system as fundamentally different kinds of places — one as a site of extraction, the other as a site of life.

The reasons are everything this chapter has named. Naples had no equivalent of the 1979 transition. The Italian state never devolved power to regional governments in the way Spain did, and the Mezzogiorno never got the political space Catalonia got. Naples has the same depth of memory as Barcelona. It did not get the institutional ground to convert that memory into governance.

Same Mediterranean. Same sun. Different fates.

Sounds like another world, doesn't it?

It is not another world. It is an alternative — accessible from this one, by routes that exist, named in this chapter. The Zapatistas have a phrase that ends *many worlds are possible*. They mean it literally. The arrangements we live under are arrangements, not laws of physics.

A wizard's job is to show you how. To name the conditions. To point at the seams. To lend the seeing that lets you recognize a possible world when you walk into one. And to show you how to reach them — the secret ways, the gathered coalitions, the slow building of the institutional ground that makes different fates possible.

Barcelona is real. So are Naples and Norfolk. The difference between them is not destiny. It is what was done with the conditions each city had.
